1:
"And he looked up, and saw the rich men casting their gifts into the treasury." The witnesses note Christ's observation of the givers at the temple treasury. The Gloss connects it to the foregoing: "our Lord having rebuked the covetousness of the Scribes who devoured widows' houses, commends the almsgiving of a widow." Bede explains the treasury: "gazophylacium is the name of the place in which money is kept" — "a chest with an opening at the top, placed near the altar, into which the priests cast all the money given for the Lord's temple." Bede draws the lesson: "as He overthrows those who trade in His house, so also He remarks those who bring gifts, giving praise to the deserving, but condemning the bad."

The witnesses note Christ "looked up" and observed the rich casting their gifts — marking how men give, not only the gift but the giver's heart.

So the witnesses make the verse the setting of the widow's mites. The lesson the expositors draw is that Christ observes and weighs the gifts men bring to God, judging not the amount only but the heart and the means of the giver. The Gloss's connection, the witnesses note, is instructive: Christ had just rebuked the scribes for devouring widows' houses under a show of religion; now he turns to commend a widow's true devotion — the contrast between the covetous hypocrite and the liberal poor. Bede's note on the treasury, the expositors observe, sets the scene: the temple had its chest for the offerings of the people, and the rich were casting in their gifts, perhaps with display. But the deeper lesson, the witnesses press, lies in Christ's looking on: as he had overthrown the traders who profaned the house, so he watches those who bring gifts to it — observing, weighing, and judging, praising the worthy and condemning the unworthy. The expositors draw the searching truth: Christ marks not merely what is given, but how and by whom; the eye that sees the rich casting in their abundance also sees the widow casting in her two mites, and judges between them by a measure the world does not use. The witnesses note the comfort and the warning: the comfort, that no gift offered to God in faith and love is unobserved by Christ, however small; the warning, that he sees through the display of the rich and the hypocrisy of the covetous to the heart behind the gift. The verse thus opens the account of the widow's mites with Christ as the watchful observer of men's offerings — preparing for the great reversal in which the poor widow's tiny gift would be valued above all the rich men's abundance, because Christ weighs the gift by the heart that gives it.
2:
"And he saw also a certain poor widow casting in thither two mites." The witnesses note the poor widow and her tiny gift. The Reformed Gill marks Christ's special regard: "whom he took particular notice of above all the rest; the poor and the widow are regarded by him... nor are their mean services, done in faith and from a principle of love, despised by him, but preferred to the greater services of others." Adam Clarke stresses her poverty: "a widow miserably poor — this is the proper import of the word, and her being miserably poor heightened the merit of the action"; the "two mites" (lepta) being the smallest coins in circulation among the Jews, two of them making a farthing.

The witnesses note the contrast: amid the rich casting in their gifts, Christ marks a poor widow whose offering was but two mites, the least of coins.

So the witnesses make the verse the entrance of the widow whose gift Christ would honour. The lesson the expositors draw is that Christ notices and values the smallest gift of the poorest giver, where it springs from faith and love. The widow, the witnesses note, was "poor" — indeed, Clarke insists, "miserably poor," destitute — and her gift was "two mites," the very smallest coins, of almost no value in themselves; in the world's eyes, a contribution beneath notice, lost amid the abundance of the rich. Yet Christ "saw also" this poor widow, the expositors press; his eye, which observed the rich, rested with special regard upon her — for, as Gill marks, the poor and the widow are his particular care, and their mean services, done in faith and love, are not despised but preferred above the greater gifts of others. The witnesses observe the heightening of the merit by her poverty: it is one thing for the rich to give much out of much, another for the destitute to give the little she has; and the smaller the means, the greater the love that gives all. The expositors draw the comfort: the poorest believer, with the least to give, is not overlooked by Christ; the widow's two mites, offered from a heart of faith, were seen, marked, and treasured by him who weighs not the amount but the love. The witnesses note the rebuke to those who measure gifts by their size: Christ's eye singles out the widow above all the rich, valuing her tiny offering above their abundance. The verse thus introduces the poor widow and her two mites — the least gift, from the least giver — that Christ might teach, in the verses to follow, the true measure of giving: not what is given, but what it costs the giver, and the love from which it flows.
3:
"And he said, Of a truth I say unto you, that this poor widow hath cast in more than they all." The witnesses note Christ's striking verdict and a distinctive of Luke. The grammatical-historical Ellicott observes that Luke's "Of a truth" (the Greek for "truly") in place of Mark's "Amen" "may be noted as characteristic" of the evangelist. Barnes cross-references the principle Paul states — that a gift is accepted "according to that a man hath, and not according to that he hath not" (2 Corinthians 8:12). The witnesses note the paradox: the widow, who cast in the least in amount, "hath cast in more than they all" — more than all the rich combined.

The witnesses note the reversal: the smallest gift declared the greatest, the poor widow's two mites surpassing the rich men's abundance.

So the witnesses make the verse Christ's verdict on the true measure of giving. The lesson the expositors draw is that God measures gifts not by their amount but by the heart and the proportion they bear to the giver's means. Christ's pronouncement, the witnesses note, is solemn — "Of a truth I say unto you" — prefacing a judgment that overturns all human reckoning: "this poor widow hath cast in more than they all." The expositors press the paradox: in the scale of coin, her two mites were the least of all the gifts; in the scale of God, they were the most — more than the combined abundance of the rich. The principle, the witnesses observe (which the next verse states and which Paul confirms, 2 Corinthians 8:12), is that the worth of a gift before God lies not in its quantity but in its proportion to what the giver has, and in the love and faith from which it flows: the rich gave much, but kept far more; the widow gave little, but kept nothing, casting in all she had. The expositors draw the lesson: God does not weigh gifts as men do, by their size, but by the self-denial and devotion they express; a small gift that costs the giver everything is greater in his sight than a large gift that costs little. The witnesses note the comfort to the poor: the believer who can give but little, yet gives it in faith and love, gives "more" in God's account than the wealthy who give much with ease; none is too poor to give greatly. The expositors observe the rebuke to the rich and the proud: their abundance, given without sacrifice, is outweighed by the widow's mites. The verse thus declares Christ's reversing verdict — the poor widow's gift the greatest of all — teaching the abiding truth that God measures the gift by the giver, the heart, and the cost; and that the least, given in love, may be the most in his sight.
4:
"For all these have of their abundance cast in unto the offerings of God: but she of her penury hath cast in all the living that she had." The witnesses note the ground of Christ's verdict: the contrast of abundance and penury. The Reformed Gill explains the rich gave "of their abundance — which they had remaining; they had large possessions, gave in much, and yet had a great deal left." The grammatical-historical Robertson marks the key word: she cast in "all her living" (bion, "livelihood," not zoēn, the principle of life) — all she had to live on. The witnesses note the decisive contrast: the rich gave a portion of their surplus; the widow gave everything she possessed.

The witnesses note the heart of the matter: the rich gave out of what they could spare, the widow gave her whole living, holding nothing back.

So the witnesses make the verse the principle behind Christ's verdict. The lesson the expositors draw is that the worth of a gift lies in what it costs the giver — in the self-denial and trust it expresses — not in its absolute size. Christ explains, the witnesses note, why the widow's two mites surpassed all the rich men's gifts: "all these have of their abundance cast in" — the rich gave from their surplus, a portion of what they had to spare, and were no poorer for the giving, retaining great possessions still. But the widow "of her penury hath cast in all the living that she had" — out of her destitution she gave everything, her whole livelihood, the very means by which she lived, keeping nothing back. The expositors press the decisive difference Robertson marks in the word: she gave not merely much in proportion, but "all her living" — the bread out of her own mouth, casting herself wholly on God for her sustenance. This, the witnesses observe, is what made her gift the greatest: not the amount, which was least, but the totality and the faith — she gave all, trusting God to provide; the rich gave a fraction, trusting still in their wealth. The expositors draw the abiding lesson: God values the gift by the cost and the love, by what is kept back as much as what is given; a gift that leaves the giver with all his abundance is small in his sight, while a gift that strips the giver of all, given in faith, is great. The witnesses note the searching application: it is easy to give of abundance and feel generous, but the true measure of devotion is the willingness to give sacrificially, even all, trusting God. The verse thus grounds Christ's verdict in the contrast of abundance and penury — the rich giving of their surplus, the widow her whole living — teaching that the greatness of a gift is measured by the self-denial and faith it embodies, and that she who gives all, though it be but two mites, gives most of all.
5:
"And as some spake of the temple, how it was adorned with goodly stones and gifts, he said..." The witnesses note the admiration of the temple and Christ's foretelling of its ruin. Eusebius recalls its beauty — "how beautiful was everything relating to the structure of the temple, history informs us" — yet Christ "proclaimed that there should not be left in it one stone upon another, for it was meet that that place, because of the presumption of its worshippers, should suffer every kind of desolation." Bede gives the providential reason: "it was ordained by the dispensation of God that the city and temple should be overthrown, lest some one yet a child in the faith, rapt in astonishment at the rites of the sacrifices, should be carried away by the mere sight of the various beauties." Ambrose draws the moral: "there is nothing made with hands which age does not impair, or violence throw down, or fire burn."

The witnesses note the contrast: men admiring the temple's splendour, Christ about to foretell its utter destruction.

So the witnesses make the verse the occasion of the prophecy of the temple's ruin. The lesson the expositors draw is the vanity of earthly magnificence and the providence that would sweep away the temple lest it hinder faith. As some, the witnesses note, spoke admiringly of the temple — "adorned with goodly stones and gifts," a marvel of beauty and wealth, the pride of the nation — Christ, far from joining their wonder, was about to foretell its complete overthrow. The expositors press the lessons the fathers draw. First, the moral Ambrose names: nothing made with hands endures; the most splendid earthly structure is subject to age, violence, and fire, and will pass away — a warning against setting the heart on what is temporal, however magnificent. Second, the providential reason Bede gives: it was ordained that the temple be destroyed, lest it become a snare to faith; for so long as the temple stood with its imposing rites and beauties, new believers might be drawn back to the old worship, carried away by the sight; its removal cut off the temptation and turned men wholly to Christ. Third, the judgment Eusebius marks: the temple's desolation was the just recompense of the presumption and wickedness of its worshippers, who had made the house of prayer a den of thieves and rejected their Messiah. The witnesses draw the lesson: the glory of the temple, on which the people gazed with pride, was doomed — its stones to be scattered, its beauty to perish — both as a judgment on the nation and as a providence that the shadow might give way to the substance, the old covenant to the new. The verse thus sets men's admiration of the temple against Christ's word of its ruin, teaching the transience of earthly splendour and the wisdom of God in removing what would hinder faith; and it opens the great discourse on the destruction of Jerusalem and the end that follows.
6:
"As for these things which ye behold, the days will come, in the which there shall not be left one stone upon another, that shall not be thrown down." The witnesses note the precise prophecy of total destruction. The grammatical-historical Robertson notes the strength of the prediction and its shock: "it was a shock to the disciples to hear this after the triumphal entry." Clarke marks the literal fulfilment: "this was literally fulfilled" in the Roman destruction. The witnesses note the completeness Christ foretells: not one stone left upon another — the utter razing of the temple they admired.

The witnesses note the total ruin foretold: every stone of the magnificent temple to be thrown down.

So the witnesses make the verse the prophecy of the temple's complete destruction. The lesson the expositors draw is the certainty and exactness of Christ's word of judgment, fulfilled to the letter in the razing of the temple. Christ, the witnesses note, answers the admiration of the temple with a startling prophecy: "the days will come, in which there shall not be left one stone upon another, that shall not be thrown down" — not partial damage, but total demolition; the whole structure, with all its goodly stones, to be cast down and scattered. The expositors press the shock this gave the disciples (Robertson notes), coming so soon after the triumphal entry, when their hopes of the kingdom's glory were high: that the temple, the centre of their nation's worship and pride, the very dwelling of God among them, should be utterly destroyed was almost unthinkable. Yet, the witnesses observe, the prophecy was fulfilled exactly: in AD 70 the Romans under Titus razed the temple, and (as history records) the stones were thrown down and the place left desolate, the prediction verified to the letter. The expositors draw the lesson: the word of Christ, whether of grace or of judgment, is sure and precise; what he foretells comes to pass, and his prophecies are confirmed by their fulfilment. The witnesses note the solemn truth this declares: that the most sacred and splendid earthly things, when they have served their purpose or provoked God's judgment, are not spared; the temple itself, holy as it was, fell under the sentence of ruin, for the shadow must give way and the guilty nation be judged. The expositors observe that this prophecy is the text of the whole discourse that follows, in which Christ unfolds the signs and circumstances of the temple's destruction and, beyond it, of his coming and the end. The verse thus records Christ's exact prophecy of the temple's total ruin — not one stone upon another — fulfilled precisely by the Romans, a proof of the certainty of his word and the occasion of the great Olivet discourse.
7:
"And they asked him, saying, Master, but when shall these things be? and what sign will there be when these things shall come to pass?" The witnesses note the disciples' question and the frame of the discourse. Cyril of Jerusalem draws the balance Christ would teach: "as to the time let no one be curious, for it is not for you to know times or seasons (Acts 1:7)... and venture not to declare when these things shall be, nor on the other hand supinely slumber; for he saith, Watch — but since it was needful to know the signs of the end... that we may not be led astray by Antichrist," Christ gives the signs. The witnesses note the twofold question: when shall these things be, and what sign shall precede them.

The witnesses note the disciples' inquiry into the time and the sign of the temple's destruction, occasioning the whole discourse.

So the witnesses make the verse the question that frames the Olivet discourse. The lesson the expositors draw is the proper balance between curiosity about times and watchfulness for the signs. The disciples, the witnesses note, hearing the prophecy of the temple's ruin, naturally ask, "when shall these things be? and what sign will there be?" — desiring to know both the time and the token of so momentous an event. The expositors press the wisdom Cyril of Jerusalem draws out in Christ's answer: on the one hand, men are not to be curious about the precise time, for it is not given them to know the times and seasons (Acts 1:7), and to fix dates is presumption; on the other hand, they are not to slumber in carelessness, but to watch, for the day will come suddenly. Between these extremes, the witnesses observe, Christ steers a middle course: he will not gratify idle curiosity with a date, but he will give the signs that should make his people vigilant and keep them from being deceived — especially from the deception of false Christs and Antichrist. The expositors note the double bearing of the disciples' question and Christ's reply: the discourse that follows speaks both of the near judgment on Jerusalem (the destruction of the temple, within that generation) and, beyond it, of the final coming of the Son of man and the end of the world — the two woven together, as is common in prophecy. The witnesses draw the lesson for the Church: not to calculate dates, which is forbidden, but to heed the signs and to watch, ready for the coming whenever it falls. The verse thus records the question — when, and by what sign — that opens the great prophetic discourse, in which Christ would teach his people to watch without curiosity, to discern the signs without fixing the time, and to be ever ready for the day of the Lord.
8:
"And he said, Take heed that ye be not deceived: for many shall come in my name, saying, I am Christ; and the time draweth near: go ye not therefore after them." The witnesses note the first warning against deceivers. The grammatical-historical Ellicott marks the text: "I am Christ" — "the word 'Christ' is an interpolation; the sentence is better left in the vagueness of the original... I am He" — the bare "I am" being significant. Clarke notes "many shall come in my name, usurping my name, calling themselves the Messiah" (cf. Matthew 24:5), and that this was fulfilled before Jerusalem's fall. Robertson glosses "be not led astray" (lest deceived). The witnesses note Christ's first counsel: beware of false Christs and false claims, and do not follow them.

The witnesses note the first sign and warning: many impostors would arise claiming to be the Messiah, and the disciples must not be deceived.

So the witnesses make the verse Christ's warning against false Christs. The lesson the expositors draw is that the first danger in the troubled times is deception, against which the disciples must be on guard. Christ's opening counsel, the witnesses note, is not a sign to be watched for so much as a peril to be avoided: "Take heed that ye be not deceived" — for the days of distress would breed impostors, "many" who would "come in my name, saying, I am" — claiming to be the Messiah, the deliverer, and drawing the people after them. The expositors observe the historical fulfilment Clarke records: before and during the troubles that preceded Jerusalem's fall, many false Christs arose, usurping the Messianic claim and leading multitudes astray (as Josephus relates). And the warning, the witnesses press, reaches beyond that time to every age of expectation: in seasons of distress and looking for deliverance, false leaders and false saviours arise, exploiting the people's hopes. Ellicott's textual note, the expositors observe, sharpens the claim: the impostors said simply "I am" — usurping the very divine name and Messianic claim — and added "the time draweth near," playing on the expectation of the kingdom to gather followers. Against all such, Christ's command is plain: "go ye not therefore after them" — do not be deceived, do not follow the false Christs and their lying promises. The witnesses draw the lesson: the first duty of the disciple in perilous times is vigilance against deception; for the great danger is not only outward calamity but inward error, the being led astray by those who claim Christ's name and authority falsely. The expositors note the abiding application: the Church must ever beware of false teachers and false saviours who arise in times of trouble, testing every claim by the word of Christ. The verse thus opens the discourse with the warning against deception — beware the false Christs, follow them not — the first and ever-needful counsel for the people of God amid the signs of the times.
9:
"But when ye shall hear of wars and commotions, be not terrified: for these things must first come to pass; but the end is not by and by." The witnesses note the warning against premature alarm. Gregory the Great draws the purpose of the forewarning: "God denounces the woes that shall forerun the destruction of the world, that so they may the less disturb when they come, as having been foreknown; for darts strike the less which are foreseen." He distinguishes the troubles: "wars refer to the enemy, commotions to citizens" — the disciples would be "troubled from within and without." Gregory notes "the last tribulation is preceded by many tribulations... that they may await that evil which has no end." The witnesses note Christ's counsel: hearing of wars, be not terrified, for these are not yet the end.

The witnesses note the warning: wars and tumults would come, but they are not the immediate sign of the end; the disciples must not be terrified.

So the witnesses make the verse Christ's caution against premature alarm at wars. The lesson the expositors draw is that wars and commotions, though they must come, are not the immediate sign of the end, and the disciple is to meet them with composure, not terror. Christ, the witnesses note, having warned against deception, now warns against fear: "when ye shall hear of wars and commotions, be not terrified" — for such upheavals, wars among nations and tumults within them, would surely come, "but the end is not by and by"; they are not the final consummation, but the long prelude to it. The expositors press the comfort in Gregory's observation: Christ foretells these woes precisely that they may disturb his people less when they come; for evils foreseen strike less sharply than those that fall unawares — "darts foreseen wound the less." The witnesses observe Gregory's distinction between "wars" (the assault of enemies from without) and "commotions" (the strife of citizens within): the disciples would be troubled on every side, from foe and from brother; yet none of this is to terrify them, for it is but the appointed forerunner of the end, not the end itself. The expositors draw the lesson against two errors: against the false Christs who would say the end was at hand to deceive, and against the panic that would read every war as the immediate consummation; the disciple is to know that these things "must first come to pass," and to wait with patience, neither deceived nor dismayed. The witnesses note the steadying of faith this affords: the believer, forewarned, is not shaken by the convulsions of the world, but recognizes them as the foretold prelude, and keeps his peace, knowing the end will come in God's time, not before. The verse thus counsels composure amid wars and tumults — be not terrified, for the end is not yet — teaching the people of God to face the upheavals of history with the calm of those who have been forewarned, and to await the end without alarm.
10:
"Then said he unto them, Nation shall rise against nation, and kingdom against kingdom." The witnesses note the foretelling of widespread war among the nations. Gill refers to the parallel (Matthew 24:7), and Barnes cross-references the prophetic word of God setting kingdom against kingdom in judgment (Haggai 2:22). The witnesses note Christ specifies the wars he had mentioned: not local conflicts only, but nation rising against nation, kingdom against kingdom — a world convulsed with war.

The witnesses note the expansion of the warning: the wars would be widespread, nation against nation, a general convulsion among the kingdoms.

So the witnesses make the verse the foretelling of international war as a sign of the troubled times. The lesson the expositors draw is that the period before the end would be marked by widespread war and convulsion among the nations, which the disciple is to recognize as foretold. Christ, the witnesses note, expands his warning of wars into a sweeping prophecy: "Nation shall rise against nation, and kingdom against kingdom" — not isolated conflicts, but a general upheaval, the peoples and powers of the earth set against one another in war. The expositors observe the fulfilment in the period leading to Jerusalem's destruction, when the Roman world was shaken by wars, rebellions, and the convulsions that attended the fall of the city and the broader turmoil of the age; and beyond that, the saying describes the troubled course of history before the final end, marked recurrently by war among the nations. The witnesses note Barnes's reference to the prophetic pattern (Haggai 2:22): God, in working out his purposes, overturns kingdoms and sets nation against nation, the wars of the earth serving, under his providence, the unfolding of his designs. The expositors draw the lesson: these convulsions, dreadful as they are, are foretold and foreknown; the disciple is not to be terrified by them (as the previous verse charged), nor to mistake them for the immediate end, but to recognize in them the signs of the times Christ described. The witnesses note the steadying truth: that even the wars of nations are within the foreknowledge and providence of God, who has foretold them to his people; nothing happens by chance, and the believer, seeing the prophecy fulfilled, may rest in the certainty of Christ's word. The verse thus foretells the widespread wars of the troubled age — nation against nation, kingdom against kingdom — as a sign foreknown and foretold, teaching the people of God to discern the times without alarm, and to trust the Lord who governs even the convulsions of the nations toward his appointed end.
11:
"And great earthquakes shall be in divers places, and famines, and pestilences; and fearful sights and great signs shall there be from heaven." The witnesses note the catalogue of physical signs and a distinctive of Luke. The grammatical-historical Ellicott notes "the mention of pestilences is, as far as the best MSS. are concerned, a feature peculiar to St. Luke," and that the Greek nouns for famine and pestilence (limos and loimos) "are all but identical in sound," giving "a rhythmical emphasis." Robertson notes "fearful sights" (literally "things of terror," phobēthra) is "only here in the New Testament," peculiar to Luke. The witnesses note the signs are not war only, but convulsions of nature — earthquakes, famines, pestilences — and terrors from heaven.

The witnesses note the expansion of the signs to the natural world: earthquakes, famines, pestilences, and fearful portents in the sky.

So the witnesses make the verse the foretelling of natural convulsions and portents. The lesson the expositors draw is that the troubled times before the end would be marked not only by wars but by upheavals of nature and terrors from heaven, all foretold by Christ. The witnesses note the catalogue: "great earthquakes... in divers places," "famines, and pestilences," and "fearful sights and great signs from heaven" — the very frame of nature shaken, the earth convulsed, men afflicted with hunger and plague, and the sky troubled with portents. The expositors observe the historical fulfilment in the period before Jerusalem's fall, when, as the historians record, earthquakes, famines, pestilences, and strange phenomena attended the troubled years; and beyond that, the saying describes the disorders that would mark the course of the age before the final consummation. Ellicott's note, the witnesses observe, marks a touch of the physician-evangelist: Luke alone (in the best texts) adds "pestilences" to the famines — fitting the doctor's eye for disease — joined to "famine" by a play on the similar-sounding Greek words. The expositors draw the lesson, consistent with the foregoing: these signs, dreadful as they are, are foretold and foreknown; the disciple is not to be terrified by them, nor to read every earthquake or plague as the immediate end, but to recognize in them the foretold tokens of the troubled times. The witnesses note the providence the verse implies: even the convulsions of nature — earthquakes, famines, pestilences, portents — are within God's foreknowledge and serve his purposes; nothing is outside his control, and the believer, seeing the signs, may rest in the certainty of Christ's word and the nearness of his coming. The verse thus extends the signs to the natural world — earthquakes, famines, pestilences, and heavenly terrors — teaching the people of God to discern the times, to face the convulsions without alarm, and to look beyond them to the coming of the Lord.
12:
"But before all these, they shall lay their hands on you, and persecute you, delivering you up to the synagogues, and into prisons, being brought before kings and rulers for my name's sake." The witnesses note the turn to the disciples' own persecution. Gregory the Great draws the order: "before all these, the deeds of wicked men are foretold... First the hearts of men, afterwards the elements, shall be disturbed, that when the order of things is thrown into confusion, it may be plain from what retribution it arises." Cyril notes the historical fulfilment: "before Jerusalem should be taken by the Romans, the disciples, having suffered persecution from the Jews, were imprisoned and brought before rulers; Paul was sent to Rome to Caesar, and stood before Festus and Agrippa." The witnesses note Christ foretells that, before the public calamities, his disciples would suffer persecution for his name.

The witnesses note the personal turn: before the great signs, the disciples themselves would be seized, imprisoned, and tried for Christ's sake.

So the witnesses make the verse the foretelling of the disciples' persecution. The lesson the expositors draw is that the followers of Christ would suffer persecution for his name before and amid the public calamities, and that this too was foreknown and foretold. Christ, the witnesses note, turns from the signs in the world to the trials of his own: "before all these, they shall lay their hands on you, and persecute you" — the disciples would be seized, haled before the synagogues and prisons, brought before kings and rulers, and all "for my name's sake." The expositors observe the historical fulfilment Cyril records: the apostles, before Jerusalem fell, suffered persecution from the Jews — imprisoned, beaten, tried; Paul stood before Festus and Agrippa and was sent to Caesar; the Acts is full of these very fulfilments. The witnesses note Gregory's observation on the order: the disturbance of men's hearts in persecution comes before the disturbance of the elements, that when the world's calamity falls it may be seen as the retribution for its sin, including its persecution of the saints. The expositors draw the lesson: persecution is the appointed lot of Christ's disciples in this world; they are to expect it, not be surprised by it, for Christ foretold it — and to suffer it "for his name's sake," as a mark of their fidelity to him. The witnesses note the dignity the phrase gives to their suffering: it is "for my name's sake" — not for any crime, but for their loyalty to Christ; their persecution is a participation in his cause, a bearing of reproach for him. The expositors observe the comfort: the trials were foreknown and foretold by Christ, who would not leave his people unwarned; and (as the next verses show) they would be turned to a testimony and attended by his help. The verse thus foretells the persecution of the disciples — seized, imprisoned, tried before rulers for Christ's name — teaching the Church to expect suffering for the gospel, to bear it as for Christ, and to know it was all foreseen by the Lord who forewarned them.
13:
"And it shall turn to you for a testimony." The witnesses note the gracious purpose in the persecution. Cyril renders the Greek vividly: "in the Greek it is, for the glory of martyrdom" — the suffering turned to honour. The Reformed Gill draws out the senses: "by this means they would have an opportunity of leaving their testimony for Christ before kings and rulers" — the persecution becoming the occasion of witness; "and what they should meet with would be a means of strengthening and confirming them"; and "be for a testimony against the rulers... before whom they should be convened." Ellicott notes the verb (literally "to come out") is used figuratively only by Luke and Paul. The witnesses note the reversal: the persecution, meant for harm, is turned to a testimony.

The witnesses note the gracious turn: what the enemies intended as persecution becomes an occasion for the disciples to testify of Christ.

So the witnesses make the verse the transformation of persecution into testimony. The lesson the expositors draw is that the sufferings of the disciples for Christ are overruled to become occasions of witness — to the gospel, to their own faith, and against their persecutors. The persecution, the witnesses note, "shall turn to you for a testimony" — God overrules the malice of the enemies so that the very trials become a platform for the gospel. Gill draws out the threefold sense, the expositors observe. First, the persecution gives the disciples opportunity to bear witness for Christ "before kings and rulers" — the courtroom becomes a pulpit, the trial an occasion to confess Christ before the great ones of the earth, as Paul preached before Felix, Festus, and Agrippa. Second, it confirms and strengthens the disciples themselves — their faith proved and deepened by suffering, their testimony made firmer. Third, it stands as a testimony against the persecutors — who, having heard the truth and rejected it, are left without excuse. Cyril's vivid rendering, the witnesses observe, lifts the suffering higher: it is "for the glory of martyrdom" — the persecution turned not to shame but to honour, the martyr's witness being the crown of fidelity. The expositors draw the comfort: the disciple need not dread persecution as mere loss, for God turns it to gain — making the trial an opportunity to glorify Christ, to confirm his own faith, and to confront his enemies with the truth. The witnesses note the abiding pattern: throughout the history of the Church, the persecution of the saints has been overruled to the spread of the gospel and the glory of God, the blood of the martyrs becoming the seed of the Church. The verse thus declares the gracious purpose hidden in persecution — it shall turn to a testimony — teaching the disciple to see in his sufferings for Christ not defeat but opportunity, the occasion of witness and the path to the glory of faithful confession.
14:
"Settle it therefore in your hearts, not to meditate before what ye shall answer." The witnesses note the counsel against anxious forethought. The Reformed Gill paraphrases: "resolve on this in your minds, and let it be a rule never to be departed from." Barnes draws out the meaning: "fix it firmly in your minds — so firmly as to become a settled principle — that you are always to depend on God for aid in all your trials." The grammatical-historical Robertson notes the word "not to meditate beforehand" (promeletan) is "the classical word for conning a speech beforehand," and Ellicott notes it "involves less anxiety" than Mark's phrase. The witnesses note Christ bids the disciples not to prepare anxiously what they will say in their defence.

The witnesses note the settled resolve Christ enjoins: not to rehearse their defence beforehand, but to trust God for the words.

So the witnesses make the verse the counsel to trust God for the defence. The lesson the expositors draw is that the disciple, brought before tribunals for Christ, is to be free from anxious forethought, casting the burden of his defence on God. Christ, the witnesses note, gives a positive command: "Settle it therefore in your hearts" — let it be a fixed, settled principle, a rule never departed from — "not to meditate before what ye shall answer." The expositors are careful to define what is forbidden: not study or diligence in general, but the anxious, fearful rehearsing of a defence before the hostile court, as though the disciple's safety depended on his own eloquence and preparation. Robertson notes the word means "conning a speech beforehand" — the careful preparation of an apology; and Christ bids them not to be consumed with such forethought, for their defence does not rest on their own resources. The witnesses observe Barnes's positive sense: the disciple is to fix it firmly in his mind that he may always depend on God for aid in his trials — to make trust in God, not anxious self-preparation, his settled habit. The expositors draw the lesson, consistent with the like promise elsewhere (Luke 12:11): the persecuted believer is to be free from the paralyzing care of how he shall answer, resting instead on the help God has promised. The witnesses note the comfort: the disciple need not dread the tribunal nor fear that his wit will fail, for God will supply (as the next verse declares) the mouth and wisdom needed. The expositors observe that this is no excuse for general negligence, but a specific promise for the crisis of persecution — that in the hour of trial, the Spirit will give what is needed. The verse thus counsels the settled resolve to trust God rather than anxious forethought — fix it in your hearts, do not rehearse your defence — teaching the persecuted disciple to cast his care on God, who has promised to be his help and his mouth before the rulers of the earth.
15:
"For I will give you a mouth and wisdom, which all your adversaries shall not be able to gainsay nor resist." The witnesses note the promise of divine eloquence and wisdom. The grammatical-historical Clarke explains: "a mouth — a ready utterance, or eloquence in speaking; they shall have abundance of wisdom to know what to say, and an irresistible eloquence to say what they ought." Ellicott draws the parallel to Moses: "the promise reminds us of that given to Moses when he drew back from the task of uttering God's message (Exodus 4:15-16); the inward faculty of thought, the outward power of uttering thought in words, should both be given." Barnes notes their adversaries "will not be able to reply to it, or resist the force of what you shall say" (cf. Acts 6:10). The witnesses note the double gift — a mouth (utterance) and wisdom (matter) — given by Christ himself.

The witnesses note Christ's promise to supply both the words and the wisdom that no adversary could withstand.

So the witnesses make the verse the ground of the foregoing counsel: the promise of Christ's own help in the hour of trial. The lesson the expositors draw is that the persecuted disciple need not prepare anxiously, because Christ himself will give him an irresistible wisdom and utterance before his judges. The reason Christ gives for the disciples' freedom from anxious forethought, the witnesses note, is this promise: "I will give you a mouth and wisdom." The expositors press the fulness of the gift, which Clarke and Ellicott draw out as twofold: "a mouth" — the outward power of utterance, a ready and even eloquent speech; and "wisdom" — the inward gift of knowing what to say, the matter as well as the manner. Both, the witnesses observe, are Christ's own gift, given in the moment of need — as God promised Moses (Exodus 4:15-16) both to be with his mouth and to teach him what to say. And the gift is irresistible: "which all your adversaries shall not be able to gainsay nor resist" — the wisdom and utterance Christ supplies would so confound the enemies that they could neither answer it nor withstand it, as the council could not resist the wisdom by which Stephen spoke (Acts 6:10). The expositors draw the comfort: the disciple, however unlearned or fearful, stands before kings and rulers not in his own strength but in Christ's, who undertakes to be his wisdom and his mouth; therefore he may be free of anxious care, confident that the help will not fail. The witnesses note the abiding fulfilment: throughout the Church's history, the simplest believers, brought before learned and powerful adversaries, have been given words of wisdom that silenced their accusers — the promise made good in every age of persecution. The verse thus grounds the counsel against anxious forethought in the sure promise of Christ's help — a mouth and wisdom no adversary can resist — teaching the persecuted to trust not their own preparation but the Lord who has pledged to speak in and through them.
16:
"And ye shall be betrayed both by parents, and brethren, and kinsfolks, and friends; and some of you shall they cause to be put to death." The witnesses note the betrayal by the nearest and the martyrdom of some. The grammatical-historical Barnes cross-references the prophetic word that a man's foes shall be those of his own household (Micah 7:5-6), and the fulfilment in the martyrs — Stephen, James, and others (Acts 7:59; 12:2; 26:10; Revelation 2:13; 6:9). Robertson notes "shall they cause to be put to death" (a causative verb) — some of the disciples would be martyred. The witnesses note the depth of the trial: betrayal not by strangers only but by parents, brethren, kinsfolk, and friends; and death itself for some.

The witnesses note the bitterest aspect of the persecution: betrayal by the nearest and dearest, and martyrdom for some.

So the witnesses make the verse the foretelling of betrayal and martyrdom. The lesson the expositors draw is that the persecution of the disciples would reach to the closest ties and even to death, yet all foreknown and foretold by Christ. The trial, the witnesses note, would be the bitterer for its source: "ye shall be betrayed both by parents, and brethren, and kinsfolks, and friends" — not by enemies and strangers only, but by the nearest and dearest, those bound by blood and love; the gospel would so divide households that a man's own family might deliver him up. The expositors observe the fulfilment of prophecy Barnes marks: Micah had foretold that a man's foes would be those of his own house (Micah 7:6), and Christ confirms it — the cost of discipleship may be the loss of family, the betrayal by those who should have stood by. And the persecution would reach its utmost: "some of you shall they cause to be put to death" — martyrdom for some, the seal of their fidelity in blood; and so it came to pass, the witnesses note, in Stephen, James, and the long line of martyrs that followed. The expositors draw the lesson: Christ does not hide from his disciples the full cost of following him; he forewarns them of betrayal and death, that they may count the cost and not be shaken when it comes. The witnesses note the steadying effect of the foreknowledge: the disciple, prepared by Christ's word, is not surprised nor overthrown when even his own family turns against him, nor when death itself is required, for he knew it was foretold. The expositors observe the tension the next verses resolve: some are to be put to death, yet "not a hair of your head shall perish" — the bodily death no real loss, the soul preserved. The verse thus foretells the bitterest trials of the disciples — betrayal by the nearest, and martyrdom for some — teaching the Church to expect that the cost of fidelity may be family and life, and to bear it as foreknown by the Lord who forewarned them, and who turns even the martyr's death to glory.
17:
"And ye shall be hated of all men for my name's sake." The witnesses note the universality of the hatred and its cause. The grammatical-historical Barnes cross-references Christ's word that the world hated his disciples as it hated him (John 17:14). The witnesses note the breadth — "hated of all men" — and the reason — "for my name's sake": the hatred falls on the disciples not for any fault, but for their attachment to Christ.

The witnesses note the comprehensive hatred: the disciples would be hated by all, for no other cause than their bearing Christ's name.

So the witnesses make the verse the foretelling of the world's hatred. The lesson the expositors draw is that the disciples of Christ would be the objects of universal hatred, not for any wrong, but solely for their loyalty to him. Christ, the witnesses note, sums up the persecution in a sweeping word: "ye shall be hated of all men" — not by some only, but generally, the disciples set against by the world; and the cause is named, "for my name's sake" — the hatred is directed at them because they belong to Christ, bear his name, and confess his gospel. The expositors press the lesson Barnes draws from John 17:14: the world hated Christ, and hates his disciples for the same reason — because they are not of the world, but witness against its sin and own a Lord it rejects; the disciple shares his Master's reproach, and the enmity that crucified Christ pursues his followers. The witnesses observe the dignity and the comfort hidden in the cause: it is "for my name's sake" — the hatred is no disgrace but an honour, a fellowship in Christ's sufferings, a mark that the disciple is truly his; to be hated for Christ is to be blessed (Luke 6:22). The expositors draw the lesson: the disciple is not to expect the world's favour, but its hatred; to be loved by all is rather a danger-sign, but to be hated for Christ's sake is the lot of the faithful. The witnesses note the steadying truth: Christ foretold this hatred, that the disciple might not be surprised nor offended when it comes, but recognize in it the fulfilment of his Lord's word and a token of his fidelity. The expositors observe that the universal hatred is yet bounded by God's care, as the next verse declares — "not a hair of your head shall perish." The verse thus foretells the world's hatred of the disciples — comprehensive, yet for Christ's name's sake — teaching the Church to expect enmity, to bear it as an honour and a fellowship with Christ, and to see in it not the failure of their cause but the proof of their belonging to the hated and rejected Lord.
18:
"But there shall not an hair of your head perish." The witnesses note the promise of preservation amid persecution and weigh its sense against the foregoing. The Reformed Gill reads it spiritually, since some were to be put to death: "this cannot be understood of entire preservation from all corporeal hurt, seeing it is before declared that they should be put to death; ... but the sense is, that though they should be betrayed, hated, persecuted, and even put to death, yet they should be no losers in the main; all things should work together for their good; and though put to death, yet that would be to their advantage." Robertson agrees it is "spiritual safety here promised, such as Paul claimed about death" (Philippians 1:21). The witnesses note the proverbial form — not a hair shall perish — denoting complete preservation of what truly matters.

The witnesses note the seeming paradox: some are to be put to death, yet "not a hair shall perish" — resolved as the safety of the soul.

So the witnesses make the verse the promise of the disciple's true preservation. The lesson the expositors draw is that, though the disciple may lose his bodily life in persecution, nothing of his real and eternal welfare shall perish. The witnesses note the apparent contradiction with the foregoing: Christ had just said some would be put to death, yet now declares "there shall not an hair of your head perish" — and the resolution, the expositors press, lies in the kind of preservation meant. It cannot be entire bodily safety, Gill argues, since martyrdom was foretold; but it is the safety of the soul and of all that truly matters — the disciple, though slain, is no loser, for his death is gain (Philippians 1:21), the gateway to eternal life. The witnesses observe the proverbial force of the phrase: "not a hair shall perish" denotes the most complete preservation, that nothing of real value shall be lost; even the death of the body, in God's reckoning, is not the perishing of the man, for the soul is kept and the body shall be raised. The expositors draw the comfort: the believer in persecution is under the perfect care of God, who so guards him that nothing can truly harm him — the worst men can do is to kill the body, and that is but to send him to glory; his real self, his soul, his eternal welfare, is untouchable. Robertson's note, the witnesses observe, grounds it in Paul's confidence: to live is Christ, to die is gain; death has no power to make the disciple a loser. The expositors observe the steadying assurance for the martyr: he may lose his head, yet not a hair shall perish; the apparent loss is the truest gain. The verse thus promises the disciple's complete preservation in the things that matter — not a hair shall perish — teaching that, though persecution may take the body, it cannot touch the soul nor rob the believer of his eternal good; and that the martyr, dying for Christ, loses nothing and gains all.
19:
"In your patience possess ye your souls." The witnesses note the call to patient endurance and weigh the readings. The Reformed Gill gives two senses: "by patiently bearing all afflictions... enjoy yourselves; possess that peace and joy in your souls which the world cannot take away"; or, taking the future reading, "by perseverance in the ways of God and the truths of Christ unto the end, ye shall be saved; shall find your lives, and enjoy your souls." Barnes reads "patience" as "perseverance in sustaining afflictions," and "possess your souls" as "preserve or keep your lives" — "by persevering in bearing these trials you will save your lives," or, more deeply, your souls. The witnesses note the keynote: patient endurance is the way to possess and preserve the soul.

The witnesses note the closing counsel of this section: by patient perseverance, the disciple keeps his soul.

So the witnesses make the verse the call to patient endurance. The lesson the expositors draw is that perseverance in patience under persecution is the way to preserve and possess the soul. Christ, the witnesses note, closes his counsel on persecution with this word: "In your patience possess ye your souls" — and the expositors weigh its rich senses. In one reading, "patience" is the patient bearing of affliction, and to "possess your souls" is to keep your inward peace and self-possession amid the storm — to enjoy, even in suffering, that peace and joy which the world cannot take away; the disciple, by patience, remains master of his own soul, unshaken and undisturbed, where impatience would lose all composure. In another (taking the future reading), the verse promises that by perseverance unto the end the disciple shall save his soul — "he that endureth to the end shall be saved" (Matthew 24:13); patient continuance in faith and obedience is the path to final salvation. The witnesses observe that both senses agree in the keynote: patience, perseverance, endurance is the grace by which the disciple holds fast his soul through persecution. The expositors draw the lesson: in the trials foretold — betrayal, hatred, death — the disciple is not to be overcome, but to endure with patience; for it is by such steadfast endurance that he keeps possession of his soul, both its present peace and its eternal safety. The witnesses note the contrast with impatience and apostasy: those who, under persecution, lose patience may lose their souls, falling away or being overwhelmed; but those who endure possess their souls, kept by the patience that grace supplies. The expositors observe that this is the fitting close to the section on persecution: having foretold the trials and promised his help and preservation, Christ calls his disciples to the one grace that will carry them through — patient endurance. The verse thus crowns the counsel on persecution with the call to patience — in your patience possess your souls — teaching that the disciple keeps his soul, its peace and its salvation, not by escaping trial but by enduring it with the steadfast patience that Christ gives.
20:
"And when ye shall see Jerusalem compassed with armies, then know that the desolation thereof is nigh." The witnesses note the sign of Jerusalem's destruction. Bede marks the transition: "hitherto our Lord had been speaking of those things which were to come to pass for forty years, the end not yet coming; He now describes the very end itself of the desolation, which was accomplished by the Roman army." Eusebius notes the finality: "by the desolation of Jerusalem He means that it was never again to be set up, or its legal rites reestablished" — no restoration as after the Babylonian captivity. Augustine connects it to Daniel's "abomination of desolation." The witnesses note the concrete sign Luke gives: the city compassed with armies — the Roman siege — as the token that desolation is near.

The witnesses note the plain sign of the temple's destruction: Jerusalem surrounded by armies, the warning to flee.

So the witnesses make the verse the sign of Jerusalem's desolation. The lesson the expositors draw is that Christ gave his people a concrete, recognizable sign by which they might know the destruction was at hand and escape it. The witnesses note Bede's observation that the discourse now turns from the general signs of the troubled times to "the very end itself" — the actual destruction of Jerusalem by the Romans; and the sign Christ gives is plain and visible: "when ye shall see Jerusalem compassed with armies, then know that the desolation thereof is nigh." Where Matthew and Mark speak of the "abomination of desolation" (Daniel's prophecy), Luke, writing for Gentiles, gives the concrete event it denoted — the encircling Roman armies. The expositors observe the mercy in this sign: it was given that the disciples might recognize the approaching ruin in time to flee (as the next verse charges), and so escape the destruction that fell on the unbelieving city; the Christians, heeding the warning, withdrew to Pella and were spared. The witnesses note Eusebius's point on the finality of the desolation: unlike the Babylonian captivity, after which the city and temple were restored, this destruction was to be final — Jerusalem never again to be the seat of the old worship, the legal rites never reestablished; the old covenant order swept away for ever. The expositors draw the lesson: God does not leave his people without warning; he gives signs by which the watchful may discern the times and escape the judgment, while the careless perish. The witnesses observe the double bearing of the discourse, which the fathers and moderns note: much in it speaks directly of Jerusalem's fall (AD 70), yet the same prophecy reaches forward, in its language and pattern, to the final coming and the end of the world. The verse thus gives the concrete sign of Jerusalem's desolation — the encircling armies — teaching that Christ forewarns his people of judgment, that the watchful may discern the sign and flee, and that the destruction of the city, final and complete, was the foretold end of the rejected nation.
21:
"Then let them which are in Judaea flee to the mountains; and let them which are in the midst of it depart out; and let not them that are in the countries enter thereinto." The witnesses note the command of urgent flight. The grammatical-historical Ellicott clarifies "the countries" as "the fields" or open country (cf. John 4:35). The witnesses note the threefold direction: those in Judea to flee to the mountains, those in the city to depart, and those in the fields not to enter — all to escape the doomed city.

The witnesses note the urgent command: at the sign, all are to flee — out of the city and away from it, not into it.

So the witnesses make the verse the command of flight from the doomed city. The lesson the expositors draw is the urgency of escape when the sign appears, and the mercy of God in providing the way of deliverance. When the encircling armies appear, the witnesses note, the disciples are not to linger but to flee at once, in three directions according to where they are: those in Judea to "flee to the mountains," seeking refuge in the hills; those "in the midst of it" — within the city — to "depart out," not trusting the city's walls; and those "in the countries" — the open fields — not to "enter thereinto," not to seek shelter in the city as men naturally would in war. The expositors press the wisdom of the command: in ordinary sieges, men flee into the fortified city for safety; but here Christ reverses the instinct, for Jerusalem itself was the place of doom, and safety lay in flight from it, not into it. The witnesses observe the historical fulfilment: the Christians, heeding this warning, fled Jerusalem before the final siege closed, withdrawing (as Eusebius and the historians record) to Pella beyond Jordan, and so escaped the horrors that fell on the city; their obedience to Christ's word was their deliverance. The expositors draw the lesson: God gives his people not only warning but direction, the way of escape from judgment; and the watchful who heed his word are saved, while the careless who ignore it perish. The witnesses note the spiritual application often drawn: as the disciples were to flee the doomed city, so the soul is to flee from the wrath to come, not lingering amid the perishing world, but escaping to the refuge God provides. The expositors observe the contrast with the unbelieving Jews, who, trusting in the city and the temple, crowded into Jerusalem and perished. The verse thus commands urgent flight from the doomed city — out and away, not in — teaching that God's warnings are to be heeded promptly, that he provides the way of escape for his people, and that the watchful who obey are delivered while the heedless are destroyed.
22:
"For these be the days of vengeance, that all things which are written may be fulfilled." The witnesses note the character of the judgment as foretold vengeance. The Reformed Gill names it "the days of God's vengeance on the Jewish nation, for their rejection and crucifixion of the Messiah." Barnes draws out the scriptural fulfilment: "judgment had been threatened by almost all the prophets against that wicked city... particularly was this destruction foretold by Daniel" (Daniel 9:26-27; cf. Deuteronomy 28; Zechariah 11:6; 14:1-2). The witnesses note the destruction was not arbitrary but the fulfilment of long-foretold judgment — "all things which are written" coming to pass.

The witnesses note the doom is named as vengeance, fulfilling the written prophecies of judgment on the city.

So the witnesses make the verse the declaration of Jerusalem's fall as foretold vengeance. The lesson the expositors draw is that the destruction of Jerusalem was the just recompense of her sin, fulfilling the long line of prophetic threatenings. Christ, the witnesses note, names the days of the siege "the days of vengeance" — not a chance calamity, but the deliberate judgment of God upon the city for her sins, chiefly her rejection and crucifixion of her Messiah, as Gill marks. The expositors press the scriptural fulfilment Barnes draws out: the prophets had long threatened judgment on Jerusalem for her crimes — Moses in the curses of the law (Deuteronomy 28), Daniel in his vision of the city and sanctuary destroyed (Daniel 9:26-27), and many others; and now, in her fall, "all things which are written" were fulfilled, the accumulated sentence of prophecy executed. The witnesses observe the solemn truth: God's judgments, though long delayed, are sure; the threatenings of his word are not idle, but come to pass in their season, and the rejection of his grace fills up the measure that brings down his vengeance. The expositors draw the lesson against presumption: Jerusalem, the holy city, was not spared because of her privileges, but judged the more severely for despising them; the days of vengeance fell on the people of the covenant who rejected their Lord, a warning that privilege abused brings the heavier doom. The witnesses note the vindication of God's word: the exact fulfilment of the written prophecies in the city's fall proves the certainty and truth of Scripture, and the righteousness of God's judgments. The expositors observe the grace that yet attends the warning: even in the days of vengeance, God provided escape for his people, who fled before the blow. The verse thus declares Jerusalem's fall as the days of foretold vengeance — the fulfilment of all that was written — teaching that God's judgments are just and sure, that the prophetic word is verified in their execution, and that the rejection of grace ends in the recompense long foretold.
23:
"But woe unto them that are with child, and to them that give suck, in those days! for there shall be great distress in the land, and wrath upon this people." The witnesses note the special woe and the greatness of the distress. The grammatical-historical Ellicott marks Luke's distinctive word: "great distress in the land — literally, great need or necessity," a word Luke uses for "tribulation," not found so in the other Gospels but used by Paul (1 Corinthians 7:26; 2 Corinthians 6:4). Barnes cross-references the horrors of the siege (Lamentations 4:10). The witnesses note the pity for mothers and the unborn — those least able to flee — and the magnitude of the coming distress and wrath.

The witnesses note the special woe upon mothers in the siege, and the great distress and wrath foretold.

So the witnesses make the verse the lament over the horrors of the siege. The lesson the expositors draw is the greatness of the calamity that would fall on Jerusalem, especially on the helpless, in the days of vengeance. Christ, the witnesses note, pronounces a special "woe unto them that are with child, and to them that give suck" — the pregnant women and nursing mothers, who, burdened and slow, could least flee the doomed city, and who would suffer most in the famine and slaughter of the siege; the woe is the voice of pity for the most helpless victims of the coming distress. The expositors observe the magnitude Christ foretells: "great distress in the land, and wrath upon this people" — the calamity would be extraordinary, a tribulation such as had not been; and the witnesses note Ellicott's mark of Luke's word for "distress" (great "necessity" or straitness), and Barnes's reference to the famine-horrors that the historians (and Lamentations 4:10) record — mothers reduced to eating their own children in the extremity of the siege. The expositors draw the lesson: the days of vengeance would bring a distress so terrible that the natural blessings of motherhood would become occasions of deeper woe; the wrath upon the people would spare not even the most helpless. The witnesses observe the solemnity of "wrath upon this people": the calamity was not mere misfortune but the wrath of God upon the nation that had rejected its Messiah — a judicial visitation, dreadful in its severity. The expositors note the compassion in Christ's word even as he foretells the doom: he weeps over the suffering, pronounces woe upon the helpless, his heart moved with pity even while justice runs its course. The verse thus laments the horrors of the siege — the woe upon mothers, the great distress, the wrath upon the people — teaching the dreadfulness of God's judgment on a guilty nation, the special suffering of the helpless, and the compassion of Christ who foretells the doom with a heavy heart.
24:
"And they shall fall by the edge of the sword, and shall be led away captive into all nations: and Jerusalem shall be trodden down of the Gentiles, until the times of the Gentiles be fulfilled." The witnesses note the fate of the people and the long treading-down of the city. The Reformed Gill records the toll: "the number of those that perished by the famine and sword was eleven hundred thousand." Lightfoot weighs the puzzling "until": "I am well assured our Saviour is discoursing about the fall of Jerusalem; but I doubt whether he touches upon the restoration of it; nor can I see great reason to affirm that the times of the Gentiles will be fulfilled before the end of the world." The witnesses note the threefold doom: slaughter by the sword, captivity among the nations, and the city trodden down by the Gentiles for an appointed period.

The witnesses note the dispersion: the people slain or scattered captive, and the city trampled by the Gentiles "until the times of the Gentiles be fulfilled."

So the witnesses make the verse the foretelling of the dispersion and the treading-down of Jerusalem. The lesson the expositors draw is the completeness of the judgment — slaughter, captivity, and the long subjection of the city — and the mysterious limit "until the times of the Gentiles be fulfilled." The witnesses note the threefold doom Christ foretells: the people would "fall by the edge of the sword" — vast multitudes slain (Gill records eleven hundred thousand perishing); the survivors "led away captive into all nations" — dispersed among the Gentiles, the long exile of the Jewish people beginning; and "Jerusalem shall be trodden down of the Gentiles" — the holy city subjected to Gentile dominion and contempt. The expositors observe the exactness of fulfilment: the slaughter, the captivity, and the centuries-long treading-down of Jerusalem by Gentile powers verify Christ's word to the letter. The phrase "until the times of the Gentiles be fulfilled," the witnesses note, is the crux: it sets a term, an appointed period, to the treading-down — yet its precise meaning is debated. Lightfoot, the expositors observe, cautiously doubts whether it points to a future restoration of the city, or whether the "times of the Gentiles" will be completed before the end of the world; he is sure Christ speaks of the fall, less sure of any restoration. Others read the "times of the Gentiles" as the period during which the gospel goes to the nations and the Gentiles are gathered in, after which (some hold, with Romans 11:25) Israel's fulness shall come. The witnesses note the openness of the phrase, which the expositors handle with reserve, not forcing a certainty Scripture leaves veiled. The verse thus foretells the dispersion and the treading-down of Jerusalem — slaughter, captivity, Gentile dominion — fulfilled in history, with the appointed limit "until the times of the Gentiles be fulfilled," a phrase that marks both the certainty of the judgment and the mystery of its bounded term in the purposes of God.
25:
"And there shall be signs in the sun, and in the moon, and in the stars; and upon the earth distress of nations, with perplexity; the sea and the waves roaring." The witnesses note the cosmic signs and their reading. Bede sets them after the times of the Gentiles, and Ambrose notes "all which signs are more clearly described in Matthew." The fathers read them of the final coming: Eusebius says "at that time when the end of this perishing life shall be accomplished... then shall succeed a new world, in which Christ Himself shall shine as a sunbeam"; Chrysostom: "as the moon and stars are dimmed by the rising sun, so at the glorious appearance of Christ shall the sun become dark." The witnesses note the convulsions in the heavens and the distress of nations on the earth.

The witnesses note the great signs — in sun, moon, and stars above, and distress and roaring seas below — heralding the coming of the Son of man.

So the witnesses make the verse the foretelling of the cosmic signs before the coming. The lesson the expositors draw is that the final coming of the Son of man would be heralded by convulsions in the heavens and distress upon the earth — the whole frame of creation shaken. The witnesses note that the discourse, having spoken of Jerusalem's fall and the times of the Gentiles, now passes (in the fathers' reading) to the signs of the final coming: "signs in the sun, and in the moon, and in the stars" — the heavenly bodies darkened or troubled; "upon the earth distress of nations, with perplexity" — the peoples in anguish and bewilderment; "the sea and the waves roaring" — the very ocean convulsed. The expositors observe the reading the fathers give: these signs herald the end of the world and the glorious appearing of Christ; and Eusebius and Chrysostom draw out a striking thought — that the darkening of sun, moon, and stars at his coming is not mere calamity but the eclipse of the lesser lights by the greater, for Christ himself shall shine as the true Sun, so that the created luminaries are dimmed by the glory of his appearing, as the stars fade at sunrise. The witnesses note the distress on earth answering the signs in heaven: the nations in perplexity, not knowing what these portents mean, gripped with dread; the sea roaring, the elements in tumult — a universal shaking of the created order before the day of the Lord. The expositors draw the lesson: the second coming will be no obscure or secret event, but heralded by signs that fill heaven and earth, terrifying the unprepared and announcing the approach of the Judge. The witnesses observe the contrast with the believer's response, which the verses to come will draw: while the nations are in distress, the redeemed are to look up. The verse thus foretells the cosmic signs of the coming — the heavens shaken, the nations in distress, the seas roaring — teaching that the day of the Son of man will be ushered in by the convulsion of all creation, the lesser lights eclipsed by the glory of the coming Christ, and the unbelieving world filled with terror at the approach of its Judge.
26:
"Men's hearts failing them for fear, and for looking after those things which are coming on the earth: for the powers of heaven shall be shaken." The witnesses note the terror that would seize men. The grammatical-historical Barnes draws out the phrase: "men's hearts failing them — the word denotes to die, and here means that the terror would be so great that men would faint and be ready to die in view of the approaching calamities." Clarke renders "men fainting away through fear, being ready to die." Barnes applies it forward: "if this was true in respect to the judgments on Judea, how much more in the day of judgment." The witnesses note the failing of hearts — men overcome with dread at the signs and at the shaking of the very powers of heaven.

The witnesses note the universal terror: men fainting with fear at the coming calamities and the shaking of the heavens.

So the witnesses make the verse the foretelling of the terror of the last days. The lesson the expositors draw is the overwhelming dread that the signs of the coming would produce in the unbelieving, and the shaking of the very framework of the heavens. The witnesses note the vividness of the description: "men's hearts failing them for fear" — and Barnes marks the strength of the word, which means to faint or die; the terror would be so great that men would swoon, their hearts giving way, ready to die with dread "for looking after those things which are coming on the earth" — in fearful expectation of the calamities they saw approaching. The expositors observe the cause: "the powers of heaven shall be shaken" — the very forces and framework of the heavens, the celestial order on which men rely, convulsed and unsettled, so that all stability seems to dissolve; and this shaking of the heavens fills the hearts of men with a terror beyond bearing. The witnesses note Barnes's forward application: if such terror attended the judgment on Judea, how much more will it grip the wicked at the final day of judgment, when they stand before the Son of God with the prospect of eternal woe, calling on the rocks to hide them (Revelation 6:16). The expositors draw the lesson: the coming of the Son of man will be a day of terror for the unprepared — the signs in heaven and earth, the shaking of all things, the approach of the Judge, filling the godless with a dread that fails the heart; for those who have no refuge in Christ, the day of his coming is a day of fainting fear. The witnesses observe the contrast with the believer, soon to be drawn: where the world faints with terror, the redeemed are to lift up their heads. The verse thus foretells the terror of the last days — men's hearts failing for fear, the powers of heaven shaken — teaching the dreadfulness of the coming for the unbelieving, and pressing every soul to find, before that day, the refuge that turns terror into hope.
27:
"And then shall they see the Son of man coming in a cloud with power and great glory." The witnesses note the visible, glorious coming of Christ. The grammatical-historical Robertson presses the timing: "as much as to say that it will be not till then; clearly the promise of the second coming of the Son of man in glory here is pictured as not one certain of immediate realization; the time element is left purposely vague." Cyril of Jerusalem gathers the testimonies of the Son's coming in glory and his session at the Father's right hand. The witnesses note the manner of the coming: visible ("they shall see"), in a cloud, with power and great glory — answering the lowliness of his first coming.

The witnesses note the climax of the discourse: the Son of man seen coming in a cloud, in power and glory.

So the witnesses make the verse the foretelling of the glorious coming of the Son of man. The lesson the expositors draw is that the discourse rises to its climax in the visible, powerful, and glorious coming of Christ, the answer to all the signs that precede it. After the convulsions of heaven and earth and the terror of men, the witnesses note, comes the great event they herald: "then shall they see the Son of man coming in a cloud with power and great glory." The expositors press the marks of this coming. It is visible — "they shall see" — not secret or obscure, but open to all, as the lightning that fills the sky; the whole world shall behold him. It is "in a cloud" — the cloud of glory, the token of the divine presence, as at the Transfiguration and the ascension (Acts 1:9). And it is "with power and great glory" — in the full majesty of his kingship, the very opposite of the lowliness of his first coming, when he came as a servant to suffer; now he comes as King and Judge, in power to subdue and glory to dazzle. Robertson's note, the witnesses observe, marks the timing: the coming is "not till then" — after the signs, after the times of the Gentiles — and its precise time is left purposely vague, that men may watch but not calculate. The expositors draw the lesson: the Son of man who was rejected, despised, and crucified will come again in glory, visible to all, before whom every eye shall look; the same Jesus, in the manner of his ascension, shall so come (Acts 1:11). The witnesses note the double aspect: terror for the unbelieving, who see their Judge approach; but, as the next verse declares, redemption for the faithful, who lift up their heads. The verse thus foretells the glorious coming of the Son of man — visible, in the cloud, with power and great glory — the climax of the discourse and the goal of all the signs, teaching the certainty of Christ's return in majesty, and pressing every soul to be ready for the day when they shall see him come.
28:
"And when these things begin to come to pass, then look up, and lift up your heads; for your redemption draweth nigh." The witnesses note the believer's response of hope. Gregory the Great draws the contrast: "having spoken against the reprobate, He now turns His words to the consolation of the elect... When the buffetings of the world multiply, lift up your heads, that is, rejoice your hearts, for when the world closes, whose friends you are not, the redemption is near which you seek." Gregory reads "lift up your heads" as "to raise up our minds to the joys of the heavenly country." The witnesses note the redeemed are to respond to the signs not with the world's terror but with hope, for their redemption draws near.

The witnesses note the believer's opposite response: while the world faints with fear, the redeemed lift up their heads in hope.

So the witnesses make the verse the word of consolation to the believer. The lesson the expositors draw is that the very signs which fill the world with terror are, for the believer, the tokens of approaching redemption, calling forth hope, not fear. The witnesses note the turn Gregory marks: having spoken of the dread of the reprobate, Christ now consoles the elect — "when these things begin to come to pass, then look up, and lift up your heads." Where the unbelieving faint with fear at the signs, the redeemed are to do the opposite: to look up, to raise their heads, to rejoice; for the same coming that is the world's terror is the believer's deliverance. The expositors press the ground of this hope: "for your redemption draweth nigh" — the coming of the Son of man, dreadful to his enemies, is the day of the believer's full redemption, the completion of his salvation, the entrance into glory; therefore the signs that herald it are to be welcomed, not dreaded. Gregory's reading, the witnesses observe, lifts the gesture into the spirit: to "lift up your heads" is to raise the mind and heart above the troubles of the closing world to the joys of the heavenly country, to rejoice that the world whose friends they are not is passing, and the redemption they long for is at hand. The expositors draw the lesson: the believer's posture toward the end is not the cowering dread of the godless but the upward look of hope; the convulsions of the last days, which terrify the world, are to him the dawn of his deliverance, the nearing of his Lord. The witnesses note the comfort this gives amid all the trials foretold — persecution, distress, the shaking of all things: the believer endures them looking up, knowing that beyond them lies redemption.
