The verse thus crowns the discourse on the coming with the word of consolation — look up, lift up your heads, your redemption draws nigh — teaching that the day which is terror to the world is hope to the faithful, and that the signs of the end are, to the redeemed, the welcome heralds of their Lord's return and their full salvation.
29:
"And he spake to them a parable; Behold the fig tree, and all the trees." The witnesses note the parable of the fig tree and the discernment it teaches. Matthew Henry draws the lesson: "Christ appoints his disciples to observe the signs of the times, which they might judge by... with as much certainty as they could judge of the approach of summer by the budding forth of the trees. As in the kingdom of nature there is a chain of causes, so in the kingdom of providence there is a consequence of one event upon another." The witnesses note Luke adds "and all the trees" to the fig tree — the lesson drawn from the budding of any tree.

The witnesses note the homely parable: as the budding trees announce summer, so the foretold signs announce the nearness of the kingdom.

So the witnesses make the verse the parable of the fig tree. The lesson the expositors draw is that the signs Christ foretold are to be read like the budding of the trees — sure tokens by which the watchful may discern the nearness of the coming. Christ, the witnesses note, turns to a homely figure: "Behold the fig tree, and all the trees" — Luke's adding "all the trees" to Mark's and Matthew's fig tree generalizes the lesson, drawing it from the budding of any tree. The expositors press the point Henry draws out: as men judge with certainty that summer is near when they see the trees shoot forth their buds and leaves, so the disciples may judge that the coming is near when they see the signs Christ has foretold come to pass; the signs are as reliable an index of the approaching event as the budding leaves are of summer. The witnesses observe Henry's deeper reflection: as in nature there is a chain of cause and effect, so in providence one event follows another in appointed sequence — when a nation fills up its iniquity, ruin is near; when the opposition to God's kingdom is removed, the kingdom gains ground; the watchful may read the signs of the times as surely as the seasons. The expositors draw the lesson: Christ would have his people discerning — not curious to fix dates, but observant of the signs, able to read the tokens of his approach and to be ready; the parable commends a spiritual attentiveness to the providential signs of the times. The witnesses note the encouragement: the signs are not given to terrify only, but to inform and prepare; as the budding tree gladdens with the promise of summer, so the signs, to the believer, herald the welcome coming of his Lord. The verse thus opens the parable of the fig tree — the budding trees the figure of the foretold signs — teaching the disciple to discern the nearness of the coming by the signs Christ gave, with the same certainty that the budding of the trees announces the approach of summer.
30:
"When they now shoot forth, ye see and know of your own selves that summer is now nigh at hand." The witnesses note the natural certainty of the figure. The grammatical-historical Robertson notes "shoot forth" and that the word is "summer" (theros), not harvest. Wesley draws the point: "ye know of yourselves — though none teach you." The witnesses note the spontaneous, self-evident knowledge: men need no instruction to know that the budding of the trees means summer is near.

The witnesses note the obviousness of the sign in nature: the budding trees self-evidently announce summer.

So the witnesses make the verse the natural ground of the parable's lesson. The lesson the expositors draw is that the signs of the coming are as plain and self-evident to the spiritually attentive as the budding trees are to all men. Christ, the witnesses note, presses the figure: "when they now shoot forth, ye see and know of your own selves that summer is now nigh at hand" — the budding of the trees is a sign so plain that men recognize it without being taught; the sight of the shooting leaves carries its own certain message that summer is near. Wesley's emphasis, the expositors observe, is on the spontaneity of the knowledge: "of your own selves — though none teach you"; no instruction is needed, for the sign is self-evident, read by every observer of nature. The witnesses press the application: so the signs Christ has foretold, when they come to pass, will be plain tokens to the watchful, as obvious in their meaning as the budding trees; the disciple who heeds them will know, as surely and as readily, that the coming is near. The expositors draw the lesson: God does not hide the signs of his approach in obscurity, but makes them, to the attentive, as legible as the changing seasons; the fault is not in the clarity of the signs but in the dullness of those who will not read them. The witnesses note the rebuke implied: men who can read the face of the sky and the budding of the trees, yet cannot discern the signs of the times (Luke 12:56), are without excuse; the spiritual signs are as plain as the natural, to those who have eyes to see. The expositors observe the encouragement to discernment: the believer is to cultivate the attentiveness that reads the providential signs as naturally as the farmer reads the season. The verse thus grounds the parable in the self-evident certainty of nature — the budding trees announcing summer of themselves — teaching that the signs of the coming are equally plain to the watchful, and that the disciple is to read them with the same ready and unprompted discernment by which all men know the approach of summer.
31:
"So likewise ye, when ye see these things come to pass, know ye that the kingdom of God is nigh at hand." The witnesses note the application and the meaning of "the kingdom of God." Clarke reads it of the gospel's advance: "after the destruction of the Jewish state, the doctrine of Christ crucified shall be preached everywhere, and everywhere prevail." Ellicott notes Luke "fills up and explains" Matthew's and Mark's simpler "It is near, even at the doors." Robertson presses: "the consummation of the kingdom is here meant, not the beginning." The witnesses note the application: as the budding trees announce summer, so the signs announce the nearness of the kingdom of God.

The witnesses note the lesson applied: the foretold signs are the tokens that the kingdom of God is near.

So the witnesses make the verse the application of the fig-tree parable. The lesson the expositors draw is that the signs Christ foretold are the sure tokens of the nearness of the kingdom of God, to be read by the watchful. Christ applies the figure, the witnesses note: "So likewise ye, when ye see these things come to pass, know ye that the kingdom of God is nigh at hand" — as the budding trees certainly announce summer, so the fulfilment of the foretold signs certainly announces the approach of the kingdom. The expositors weigh the sense of "the kingdom of God" here. In one bearing, the witnesses observe with Clarke, it points to the great advance of Christ's kingdom after the fall of Jerusalem — the old order swept away, the gospel going forth to all nations and prevailing; the destruction of the Jewish state being the occasion of the kingdom's wider spread. In another, fuller bearing, Robertson notes, it means "the consummation of the kingdom" — its final, glorious completion at the coming of the Son of man, when the kingdom is brought to its perfection. The witnesses note that the discourse, holding together the near and the far, allows both: the signs announced both the nearer establishment of the kingdom in power after Jerusalem's fall and the final consummation at Christ's return. Ellicott observes that Luke, the expositors note, expands the simpler saying of the other Gospels ("near, even at the doors") into this fuller form, explaining what is near — the kingdom of God. The expositors draw the lesson: the believer is to read the signs of the times as heralds of the kingdom's advance and consummation, and so to live in expectation and readiness, knowing that the kingdom for which he prays and waits draws ever nearer. The verse thus applies the parable — the signs announcing the kingdom as the budding trees announce summer — teaching the disciple to discern in the events of providence the nearing of the kingdom of God, both in its advance through the gospel and in its final consummation at the coming of the Lord.
32:
"Verily I say unto you, This generation shall not pass away, till all be fulfilled." The witnesses note the famous and difficult saying. The grammatical-historical Robertson lays out the debate frankly: "the words give a great deal of trouble to critics. Some apply them to the whole discourse including... the second coming and the end of the world" — and of these, some say "Jesus was simply mistaken in his eschatology," others that "he has not been properly reported." But "others apply them only to the destruction of Jerusalem, which did take place in A.D. 70 before that generation passed away." Clarke reads "this generation" as "this race of men" (the Jews), or the men then living. The witnesses note the saying sets a near limit — "this generation shall not pass" — to the fulfilment of "all these things."

The witnesses note the crux: "this generation shall not pass till all be fulfilled" — resolved by referring it to the destruction of Jerusalem.

So the witnesses make the verse the great interpretive crux of the discourse. The lesson the expositors draw is that the saying, rightly understood, refers to the destruction of Jerusalem, which the generation then living did indeed see — and so vindicates the truth of Christ's word against those who would charge him with error. The witnesses note Robertson's frank survey of the difficulty: some take "this generation shall not pass away, till all be fulfilled" of the whole discourse, including the second coming and the end of the world — and, since that did not come within the generation, are forced to say either that Jesus erred, or that the Gospels misreport him. But the expositors press the better and historic reading, which Robertson himself favours: the saying refers, in the main, to the destruction of Jerusalem and the events surrounding it — "all these things" that the disciples had asked about — which did, in fact, come to pass before that generation passed away, in AD 70, within forty years. On this reading, the witnesses observe, the prophecy was fulfilled exactly: the generation then living saw the temple destroyed, the city compassed and razed, the people scattered — all as Christ foretold; and so his word stands vindicated, not impugned. Clarke's note, the expositors observe, allows also that "this generation" may mean "this race" — the Jewish people, who would not pass away till all was fulfilled, and indeed survive to this day. The witnesses note the orthodox handling of the crux: the language of the discourse blends the near (Jerusalem's fall) and the far (the final coming), as prophecy often does; but the time-limit "this generation" attaches to the near fulfilment, which came to pass as promised, while the final coming remains, its time purposely vague (as v.27 showed). The expositors draw the lesson against the sceptic: there is no error in Christ's word; the generation saw all that was promised it, and the certainty of the near fulfilment is the pledge of the far. The verse thus states the difficult saying — this generation shall not pass till all be fulfilled — which the faithful interpreter refers to the destruction of Jerusalem, fulfilled within the generation, vindicating the truth of Christ's prophecy and leaving the final coming to its appointed, unrevealed hour.
33:
"Heaven and earth shall pass away: but my words shall not pass away." The witnesses note the supreme certainty of Christ's word. The grammatical-historical Robertson marks the emphatic future ("shall not pass away") and notes "it is noteworthy that Jesus utters these words just after the difficult prediction in verse 32" — staking the permanence of his word on the truth of his prophecy. Barnes cross-references the prophets' like declaration of the abiding word of God (Isaiah 40:8; 51:6). Cyril of Jerusalem expounds the passing of the world: "this world passeth away, that the fairer world may be made manifest" (Isaiah 34:4). The witnesses note the contrast: heaven and earth, the most stable things men know, shall pass; Christ's words shall not.

The witnesses note the great assurance: the very heavens and earth shall pass away, but the words of Christ never.

So the witnesses make the verse the seal of certainty upon Christ's word. The lesson the expositors draw is that the words of Christ are more sure and enduring than the very frame of creation. The witnesses note the force of the contrast: "Heaven and earth shall pass away" — the heavens and the earth, which men take to be the most stable, permanent, and enduring of all things, shall yet pass; the created order is not eternal, but shall be dissolved and remade. "But my words shall not pass away" — Christ's words, by contrast, are absolutely enduring; they shall outlast heaven and earth, never failing, never falsified, standing for ever. The expositors press the placing of this saying, which Robertson marks: Christ utters it "just after the difficult prediction" of verse 32, deliberately staking the permanence of his word upon the certainty of the prophecy he has just given — as if to say, the destruction of Jerusalem and all I have foretold shall as surely come to pass as that my words cannot fail. The witnesses observe the comfort and the warning: the comfort, that every promise of Christ is sure, more certain than the ground beneath our feet or the sky above — the believer may rest his whole hope on the unfailing word; the warning, that every threatening of Christ is equally sure — the judgments he foretold will come, his word of warning as enduring as his word of promise. Barnes's reference to the prophets, the expositors note, sets Christ's claim in the line of Scripture's witness to the abiding word of God (Isaiah 40:8) — but here Christ claims it for his own words, a claim of deity, for only God's word stands for ever. Cyril of Jerusalem, the witnesses observe, draws out the passing of the world as the prelude to the fairer world to come. The expositors draw the lesson: the disciple is to build on the words of Christ as on the only unshakable foundation; heaven and earth may go, but the word of his Lord abides. The verse thus seals the discourse with the supreme certainty of Christ's word — more enduring than heaven and earth — teaching that all he has foretold shall surely come to pass, that his promises and threatenings alike are unfailing, and that the word of Christ is the one sure ground for faith amid a passing world.
34:
"And take heed to yourselves, lest at any time your hearts be overcharged with surfeiting, and drunkenness, and cares of this life, and so that day come upon you unawares." The witnesses note the call to watchfulness against worldly excess. Theophylact names the remedy: "against which the only remedy is watching and prayer." Basil draws the lesson of self-watch: "Christ has given us this warning, that what comes to brute animals by nature may be ours by reason and prudence — that we may flee from sin as the brutes shun deadly food... Take heed to yourselves, that is, look around you on all sides, keeping an ever-watchful eye to the guard of the soul." The witnesses note the threefold danger — surfeiting (excess), drunkenness, and the cares of this life — which overcharge the heart and leave the soul unprepared.

The witnesses note the warning against the heart being weighed down by indulgence and worldly care, lest the day come unawares.

So the witnesses make the verse the call to watchfulness against worldly excess. The lesson the expositors draw is that the believer must guard his heart against the indulgence and care that dull the soul and leave it unprepared for the coming day. Christ, the witnesses note, turns from prophecy to exhortation: "take heed to yourselves" — a charge of vigilant self-watch — "lest at any time your hearts be overcharged" — weighed down, burdened, made gross and heavy — "with surfeiting, and drunkenness, and cares of this life." The expositors observe the threefold peril: surfeiting, the excess of pleasure and indulgence; drunkenness, the stupefying of the senses; and the cares of this life, the anxious absorption in worldly business — all of which, the witnesses note, deaden the soul, dull its watchfulness, and bind the heart to earth, so that "that day" — the coming of the Lord, or the day of death and judgment — comes "upon you unawares," finding the soul heavy, sluggish, and unprepared. Basil's exhortation, the expositors press, draws the lesson of self-watch: the believer is to keep an ever-watchful eye on the guard of his soul, fleeing sin as the brute shuns deadly food, by reason and prudence; he is to look on every side, lest he be surprised. Theophylact names the remedy, the witnesses observe — watching and prayer — which the verses to come will press. The expositors draw the application: the danger is not only gross sin (drunkenness) but the subtler perils of overindulgence and worldly care, which, no less than open vice, overcharge the heart and unfit it for the coming; the disciple must guard against being so absorbed in pleasure or in business that he forgets the day. The witnesses note the suddenness the warning presses: the day comes unawares on the unprepared, as a snare (the next verse); only the watchful escape surprise. The verse thus calls the believer to vigilant self-watch against surfeiting, drunkenness, and worldly care — the threefold weight that dulls the soul — teaching that readiness for the coming day requires a heart kept light and watchful, free from the indulgence and absorption that would leave it unprepared.
35:
"For as a snare shall it come on all them that dwell on the face of the whole earth." The witnesses note the suddenness and universality of the coming. The Reformed Gill draws out the figure: "as a snare, in which a bird is suddenly taken, and cannot get out again... so the destruction of the Jewish nation should be sudden and unavoidable, and universal." Barnes notes the snare denotes suddenness — "birds are caught by a snare; it is sprung on them quickly, when they are not expecting it" (Psalm 69:22; Isaiah 24:17; 1 Thessalonians 5:2). Clarke reads "the whole earth" as "this whole land" of Judea. The witnesses note the day comes as a snare — suddenly, unavoidably, upon all the careless.

The witnesses note the day's coming as a snare: sudden, inescapable, upon all the unprepared.

So the witnesses make the verse the warning of the day's sudden and universal coming. The lesson the expositors draw is that the day of the Lord will come suddenly and inescapably upon the careless, as a snare upon a bird. The witnesses note the figure Christ uses: "as a snare shall it come" — the day will spring upon men as a trap springs on a bird, suddenly, when they are not expecting it, and once sprung, inescapable; the careless, like the heedless bird, are caught before they are aware, with no time to flee. Gill draws out the threefold force of the snare, the expositors observe: it is sudden — coming when least looked for; unavoidable — once it falls, there is no escape; and universal — sprung "on all them that dwell on the face of the whole earth," sparing none of the unprepared. The witnesses note the scope: whether read of the destruction of Jerusalem (which fell suddenly on the whole land of Judea, as Clarke takes it) or of the final coming (upon the whole earth), the lesson is the same — the day comes as a snare on the careless, everywhere. The expositors draw the connection to the foregoing warning: it is precisely because the day comes so suddenly and inescapably that the believer must "take heed" and watch, lest his heart be overcharged and the snare catch him unawares; the suddenness is the reason for the vigilance. The witnesses observe the contrast between the watchful and the careless: the snare catches the unprepared, but the watchful, forewarned, escape — as the next verse promises, those who watch and pray shall be accounted worthy to escape and to stand. The expositors note the universality as a warning to all: none may presume on exemption; the day will come on all the dwellers of the earth, and only watchfulness distinguishes the escaping from the ensnared. The verse thus presses the sudden, inescapable, universal coming of the day — as a snare on all the careless — teaching that, because the day springs unawares on the unprepared, the disciple must watch and pray, that he be not caught in the snare but found ready when it comes.
36:
"Watch ye therefore, and pray always, that ye may be accounted worthy to escape all these things that shall come to pass, and to stand before the Son of man." The witnesses note the double duty of watching and praying. The Reformed Gill expounds: "watch against every sin, snare, and temptation... pray continually, for fresh supplies of grace." Barnes reads "to stand before the Son of man" as "denoting approbation, acquittal, favour" — to be accepted at his coming, while others are condemned (Psalm 1:5; Revelation 6:17). The witnesses note the twofold remedy against the snare: constant watchfulness and unceasing prayer, that the believer may escape the judgment and stand accepted before Christ.

The witnesses note the charge: watch and pray always, that the believer may escape the coming woes and stand before the Son of man.

So the witnesses make the verse the call to watch and pray. The lesson the expositors draw is that constant watchfulness and unceasing prayer are the appointed means by which the believer escapes the coming judgment and is found accepted before Christ. Christ, the witnesses note, draws the practical conclusion from the suddenness of the snare: "Watch ye therefore, and pray always" — the twofold duty of the waiting disciple. To "watch," Gill explains, is to be ever on guard against sin, snare, and temptation, keeping the soul awake and vigilant, not overcharged with surfeiting or care; and to "pray always" is to be in continual dependence on God, seeking fresh supplies of grace, that the heart be kept and strengthened. The expositors press the union of the two: watching without prayer is presumption, prayer without watching is idleness; together they are the believer's safeguard against the snare. The end Christ names, the witnesses observe, is twofold: "that ye may be accounted worthy to escape all these things" — to be delivered from the judgments and woes that shall fall on the careless world; "and to stand before the Son of man" — which Barnes reads as to be approved, acquitted, accepted at his coming, standing with favour before the Judge while the wicked are condemned and call on the rocks to hide them. The expositors draw the lesson: the way to be ready for the coming, to escape its terrors and to stand accepted, is not by calculating its time but by watching and praying continually — a life of vigilance and dependence that keeps the soul prepared whenever the day falls. The witnesses note the comfort and the spur: the watchful and praying believer is promised escape and acceptance, while the careless are ensnared; the means are within reach of every disciple, and the issue is eternal. The verse thus crowns the call to readiness with the double duty of watching and praying — that the believer may escape the woes and stand before the Son of man — teaching that constant vigilance and unceasing prayer are the way to be found ready, accepted, and safe at the coming of the Lord.
37:
"And in the day time he was teaching in the temple; and at night he went out, and abode in the mount that is called the mount of Olives." The witnesses note Christ's example of diligence in his last days. Bede draws the lesson: "what our Lord commanded in word, He confirms by His example; for He who bid us watch and pray before the coming of the Judge... as the time of His Passion drew near, is Himself instant in teaching, watching, and prayer." Theophylact notes the evangelists record but a fraction of his teaching: "whereas He preached nearly three years, all the teaching they have written down would scarcely suffice for the discourse of a single day... extracting a few, they have given only a taste of the sweetness of His teaching." The witnesses note Christ's pattern: teaching in the temple by day, retiring to the Mount of Olives by night.

The witnesses note Christ's own diligence — teaching by day, watching and praying by night on Olivet — confirming his command by his example.

So the witnesses make the verse the record of Christ's diligent example. The lesson the expositors draw is that Christ practised the watchfulness and devotion he commanded, leaving his disciples a pattern in his last days. The witnesses note the diligence of these final days: "in the day time he was teaching in the temple" — pouring out his teaching to the people, instant in his work, not slackening as the cross drew near but labouring to the end; "and at night he went out, and abode in the mount that is called the mount of Olives" — retiring not to ease but to the mount of prayer and watching, where he was wont to spend the night in communion with the Father. Bede's observation, the expositors press, draws the lesson: Christ confirmed his command by his example; he who bade his disciples watch and pray before the coming of the Judge was himself, as his Passion approached, instant in teaching, watching, and prayer — practising the very vigilance and devotion he enjoined. The witnesses observe the model this sets: the disciple is to be diligent in his work by day and devout in prayer by night, as his Master was; the call to watch and pray is enforced by the Lord's own example. Theophylact's note, the expositors observe, adds a touching reflection: the evangelists have recorded but a small portion of Christ's teaching — a mere taste of its sweetness — for he taught far more than could be written; the few discourses preserved are a sample of the abundance. The witnesses draw the lesson: Christ's example in these last days — teaching by day, watching by night — is the pattern for his people, a life of diligent labour and constant devotion, especially as the end approaches. The verse thus records Christ's diligent example — teaching in the temple, watching on Olivet — confirming by his practice the command he gave, and leaving his disciples a model of the watchfulness and prayer by which they too are to await the coming of the Lord.
38:
"And all the people came early in the morning to him in the temple, for to hear him." The witnesses note the people's eager attendance on Christ's teaching. The Reformed Gill notes "all the people" is to be taken largely — "a large number" — who "some out of curiosity, and others from a real love to him and his ministry, rose betimes, and came early." Ellicott notes the Greek verb for "came early" is one used in the Old Testament for rising early to seek God (cf. Job 8:5; Jeremiah 25:3). The witnesses note the eagerness: the people rising early and crowding to the temple to hear Christ, in these last days before his passion.

The witnesses note the people's eager devotion: rising early to hear Christ teach in the temple.

So the witnesses make the verse the close of the chapter, marking the people's eagerness to hear Christ. The lesson the expositors draw is the value of Christ's teaching and the example of those who rose early to seek it, even as his enemies plotted his death. The witnesses note the picture: "all the people came early in the morning to him in the temple, for to hear him" — the multitude, in these final days, crowding to the temple at dawn, eager to catch the words of Christ; the same people whose devotion (as the previous chapter noted) had restrained the rulers' malice. Gill marks the mixed motives, the expositors observe — some drawn by curiosity, others by real love for Christ and his ministry — yet all so eager that they "rose betimes, and came early," not willing to miss his teaching. Ellicott's note on the word for "came early," the witnesses press, is suggestive: it is the very word used in the Old Testament for rising early to seek God (Job 8:5; Jeremiah 25:3) — so that the people's early coming to hear Christ is, as it were, a seeking of God betimes, a diligent and eager pursuit of his word. The expositors draw the lesson: Christ's teaching was so precious that the people rose early to hear it; and their example commends to every believer a like eagerness for the word of God — to seek it early, diligently, and with love. The witnesses note the contrast that closes the chapter: while the rulers sought to destroy Christ, the people thronged to hear him; while his enemies plotted in the dark, the multitude came at dawn to his light. The expositors observe the providence: this very eagerness preserved Christ for his appointed hour, the people's devotion staying the hands of his foes. The verse thus closes the chapter with the people's eager attendance — rising early to hear Christ in the temple — teaching the preciousness of his word, commending the diligence of those who seek it betimes, and marking, in the calm before the storm, the love of the people that surrounded the Saviour in his last days of teaching before the cross.
